questions.text: I hold the view that self-investment yields the highest returns of any investment.
I prefer to ask questions that help someone see for themselves whether their approach is working.
I am at my best when I am a trusted co-pilot.
I find that improvisation gives me more confidence than any methodology in uncertain situations.
I routinely choose the option that benefits others, even when a different choice would benefit me more.
I am quick to declare a path forward when the group is circling.
I find that I often need to be told directly when I have accidentally offended someone.
I believe that investing in someone else's potential is more important than polishing my own resume.
I believe that my personal development plan should be revisited and refined regularly.
I like to be the one who determines the scope of what the team works on.
I find that the emotional atmosphere of a group doesn't easily pull me into its current.
I notice when someone matches the volume of the loudest person in the room to assert themselves.
I notice when someone uses the phrase 'to be honest' as a signal that previous statements may not have been.
I start new projects whenever inspiration strikes, even if others are unfinished.
I believe my skills and abilities exist primarily to serve the people around me.
I prefer to deal with what is said rather than speculate about what was meant.
I can remain focused and clearheaded even in high-pressure situations.
I experience emotional triggers as a kind of switch that flips instantly.
I make sure to have the right tools and materials ready before starting any task.
I enjoy the pressure that comes with being the person in charge.
I hold the view that shortcuts usually cost more in the long run than doing things properly.
I notice when someone's laugh does not quite reach their eyes.
I hold the view that composure in the presence of suffering is a form of reliability that makes a practical difference in outcomes.
I hold the view that I owe it to myself to pursue the life I want.
I experience the end of something meaningful with a sense of completion rather than devastation.
I am usually the first to check in with the group before anyone takes charge.
I hold the view that structured reasoning about pain and hardship is a more scalable approach to doing good than emotional responsiveness.
I hold the view that the moral concern I experience for those in suffering is the quality that makes my engagement with the world morally serious.
I think that a rational, structured approach to suffering is the method that most effectively respects the complexity of the situations in which people find themselves.
I finish unpleasant but necessary tasks promptly so they don't hang over me.
My organizational system makes sense to me even if it would confuse someone else.
I believe straightforward communication is more productive than nuanced signaling.
I believe that moral engagement with suffering is most meaningful when it persists through difficulty, fatigue, and competing demands.
My body seems to store emotional charge that I can feel building over the course of a stressful week.
I believe that the felt weight of another's pain is a form of moral knowledge that cannot be replaced by abstract reasoning alone.
I notice I carry physical tension that maps directly onto whatever I'm feeling emotionally.
